rationale: This reaction is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (sulfuric acid) catalysis. This carbocation is then trapped by a simple nitrile, acetonitrile, which after hydrolysis yields the corresponding N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and products are simple, acyclic, and contain minimal functional groups to clearly illustrate the core transformation.
collision_avoidance_note: The use of simple, acyclic aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) avoids motifs common in other named reactions. Specifically, it avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts, etc.), complex ring systems, and other reactive functional groups that could lead to ambiguity with reactions like the Beckmann rearrangement (which involves oximes) or the Schmidt reaction (which involves azides).
